# Command reference

Generated from each module's `module.json` and workflow bodies
(`modules/<module>/workflows/<id>.md`). Regenerate with `node scripts/gen-command-docs.mjs`.
Step headings below index the flows — the workflow bodies themselves are the full,
authoritative instructions.

## openspec-backlog

Ordered backlog ledger + working briefs + autonomous lifecycle runner (v0.2.0)

| Id | Name | Description | Argument hint |
|---|---|---|---|
| `backlog` | OPSX+: Backlog | Turn messy context into ordered, deduplicated backlog items - a ledger row plus lite working brief per item | `free-form context, goals, or research to capture` |
| `brief` | OPSX+: Brief | Deepen one backlog item's working brief at pick-up time - codebase-grounded requirements, risks, pointers; deep mode adds web research | `<item-name> [deep \| best practices \| deep research]` |
| `next` | OPSX+: Next | Autonomously process exactly ONE backlog item end-to-end - brief, propose, apply, verify, sync, archive; designed for /loop and /goal | `(no arguments — picks the next eligible item)` |

### /opsx:backlog

**Input**: The argument after `/opsx:backlog` is free-form context to capture — goals, research notes, a PRD, audit findings — or nothing, in which case work from what the conversation surfaced (when invoked as a skill, take the equivalent from the user's request).

Steps:

1. Read the conventions and the ledger first
2. Extract the ideal end-state, then run a light research pass
3. Dedup against all three surfaces
4. Split the 0→1 path into right-sized items
5. Create a lite brief per NEW item
6. Insert rows in dependency-and-value order
7. Revise existing Upcoming rows where new context changes the picture

### /opsx:brief

**Input**: The argument after `/opsx:brief` is the item name (a kebab-case ledger row), optionally followed by a deep-mode keyword — `deep`, `best practices`, or `deep research` (when invoked as a skill, take the equivalent from the user's request).

Steps:

1. Read the conventions, then locate the item
2. Check OpenSpec state
3. Investigate the codebase (read-only)
4. Refine the brief in place
5. Deep mode (only when the argument carries a deep keyword)
6. Challenge the depth
7. Split, or spawn follow-on waypoints, if the shape demands it
8. Update the row's pointer state

### /opsx:next

**Input**: The argument after `/opsx:next` is empty — this command takes no arguments (when invoked as a skill, take the equivalent from the user's request). The ledger alone picks the item; nothing in the request overrides its order.

Steps:

0. Read the ground rules
1. Pick the item — from the ledger, nothing else
2. Brief (pointer `—` or lite brief)
3. Propose (pointer `briefs/<name>.md`)
4. Apply (pointer `openspec/changes/<name>/`)
5. Verification gate
6. Sync, then archive
7. Iteration report

## openspec-pillars

Timeless belief pillars + controlled vocabulary resolver + the delivery-depth discipline (v0.2.0)

| Id | Name | Description | Argument hint |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pillar` | OPSX+: Pillar | Create or evolve a pillar in openspec/pillars/ — guided authoring in the 5-section format, with loud, owner-gated meaning changes | `<slug or belief> (existing slug evolves; new belief creates; empty lists pillars)` |
| `definition` | OPSX+: Definition | Add or update an entry in openspec/pillars/DEFINITIONS.md — the controlled vocabulary resolver (nouns only; never coin an undefined word) | `<term> [alias, meaning, or pointer context to capture]` |

### /opsx:pillar

**Input**: The argument after `/opsx:pillar` is the pillar to work on (when invoked as a skill, take the equivalent from the user's request). Could be: …

Steps:

1. Read the ground rules first
2. Decide: new pillar or evolution
3. Author in the 5-section format
4. Wire it into the README
5. Meaning changes are LOUD
6. Vocabulary check

### /opsx:definition

**Input**: The argument after `/opsx:definition` is the term to define or maintain (when invoked as a skill, take the equivalent from the user's request). Could be: …

Steps:

1. Read the DEFINITIONS.md header first
2. Locate or create the entry
3. Write the entry in the exact format
4. Pick the right maintenance move
